content.text: A Cowork scheduled task runs automatically on a repeating interval — daily, weekly, or monthly — without manual triggering. The task goal is written once and executed each cycle, pulling fresh data from connectors or the workspace on each run. Scheduled tasks are how you automate recurring knowledge work: weekly status reports, daily metric digests, monthly client summaries, and ongoing data monitoring.
How Scheduled Tasks Work

attrs.alt: Scheduled task lifecycle: definition triggers execution which writes workspace output, then advances to the next cycle

content.text: A scheduled task is a project task with a recurrence setting. When the schedule fires, Cowork assembles the full project context (memory, workspace files, connector permissions) and executes the goal from start to finish. The goal is static — the same text runs every cycle — but the data it operates on changes because connectors fetch fresh information and workspace files can be updated between runs.

The workspace is the continuity layer across runs. Output from run N persists in /workspace/ and is visible to run N+1. This enables patterns like trend analysis (compare this week to last week) and incremental processing (only handle records not yet seen in prior runs).
Setting Up a Scheduled Task
Scheduled tasks are configured in the Cowork project interface. The key decisions are frequency, anchor time, and goal design.
Frequency Options
Frequency
Typical Use Case
Runs Per Month
Daily
Metric digest, log monitoring, inbox triage
Weekly
Status reports, team summaries, pipeline reviews
Bi-weekly
Sprint reviews, client touchpoints
Monthly
Executive reports, invoicing summaries, trend analysis
Anchor Time

The anchor time determines when in the day (and week, for weekly schedules) the task fires. Choose an anchor time after the source data is reliably available. For example, if a data pipeline runs at 6 AM and produces the source CSV, schedule the Cowork task for 7 AM to ensure the file is complete before the task reads it.
Cowork scheduled tasks run in UTC. Convert your local anchor time to UTC when configuring the schedule to avoid daylight-saving-time drift.
Designing a Scheduled Task Goal

The goal for a scheduled task must be written to work correctly every time it runs, including the first run and runs where source data may be missing or incomplete. Goals that are too specific to one run's conditions will fail on subsequent cycles.
Stateless Goals (No Cross-Run Dependency)
The simplest scheduled task goal is stateless: it reads the current data, processes it, and writes an output — with no reference to prior runs. Each execution is independent.

Fetch the current open Jira issues in project ANALYTICS where priority = High
(connector: Jira).

For each issue, extract: issue key, title, assignee, days since created.

Produce a markdown table sorted by days since created (oldest first).
Output to /workspace/outputs/high-priority-issues.md

Overwrite the file on each run — do not append.
Stateful Goals (Cross-Run Comparison)
A stateful goal reads the output of a prior run from /workspace/ and uses it to compute a delta. This enables trend tracking, change detection, and incremental processing.

This task runs weekly. Prior week's output is at /workspace/outputs/weekly-metrics.md.

Step 1: Read /workspace/outputs/weekly-metrics.md (the prior run's data).
If the file does not exist, treat all metrics as baseline (no prior comparison).

Step 2: Fetch this week's metrics from Google Analytics (connector: Google Analytics,
dimensions: sessions, pageviews, bounce_rate, date_range: last 7 days).

Step 3: Produce a markdown report with:
- This week's metrics table
- WoW change for each metric (vs prior run data; omit if no prior exists)
- Flag any metric with WoW change > 20% in either direction

Step 4: Overwrite /workspace/outputs/weekly-metrics.md with this week's data only
(not the comparison — the next run will use it as the new baseline).

The key discipline in stateful goals: write only the current period's data to the baseline file. If you include comparison commentary in the baseline, the next run's diff will be comparing commentary to raw data — a mismatch.
Incremental Processing Goals
An incremental goal processes only records that have not been handled in prior runs. It uses a state file to track the watermark (last-processed ID, timestamp, or count).

This task runs daily. The watermark file is /workspace/state/last_processed_id.txt.

Step 1: Read /workspace/state/last_processed_id.txt.
If it does not exist, use 0 as the starting ID.

Step 2: Fetch Jira issues created after ID {last_processed_id}
(connector: Jira, filter: id > last_processed_id, order: ascending).

If no new issues are found, write 'No new issues since ID {last_processed_id}'
to /workspace/outputs/daily-new-issues.md and stop.

Step 3: For each new issue, extract key, title, assignee, priority, created date.
Append to /workspace/outputs/all-issues-log.md (do not overwrite — append).

Step 4: Overwrite /workspace/state/last_processed_id.txt with the highest
ID seen in this run.
Workspace Layout for Scheduled Tasks
A consistent workspace structure prevents output overwriting and makes it easy to trace what each run produced.

/workspace/
  inputs/
    source.csv          <- updated externally before each run (optional)
  outputs/
    latest.md           <- current run output; overwritten each cycle
    2024-06-30.md       <- archived prior run (written by the goal)
    2024-06-23.md
  state/
    last_processed_id.txt  <- watermark for incremental tasks
    last_run_meta.json     <- metadata about the prior run
  logs/
    run-log.md          <- append-only log of each execution summary
Archiving Prior Outputs
If you need a history of every run's output, have the goal write to both a date-stamped file and a 'latest' file. The date-stamped file archives the run; the latest file is always the most recent.

# Goal pattern for archiving:
Write the output report to:
  /workspace/outputs/latest.md (overwrite)
  /workspace/outputs/{YYYY-MM-DD}.md (create, do not overwrite)

If /workspace/outputs/{YYYY-MM-DD}.md already exists
(re-run on the same day), append a suffix: {YYYY-MM-DD}-2.md
Handling Missing or Stale Data
Scheduled tasks run regardless of whether source data is fresh. A robust goal handles the case where connectors return no data or workspace files are not updated on schedule.
Scenario
Goal Behavior
Example Instruction in Goal
Connector returns empty results
Note the absence explicitly; do not error
If no issues returned, write 'No issues found this cycle' and stop
Workspace source file unchanged
Detect and note; optionally skip processing
If /workspace/inputs/source.csv has the same size as last run, note 'source unchanged' in output
Connector API rate limit or error
Write a partial output noting the failure
If connector call fails, write 'Connector unavailable' in the output header and process whatever was retrieved
Prior run baseline file missing (first run)
Treat as baseline; skip comparison
If /workspace/outputs/latest.md does not exist, this is a baseline run — output data without WoW comparison
Scheduled Tasks as Pipeline Stages

Multiple scheduled tasks in the same project can form a pipeline: one task prepares data, another analyzes it, a third distributes the result. Because all tasks share the same workspace, the output of one task is the input of the next.

# Pipeline example: three scheduled tasks

# Task 1 — runs daily at 7 AM (data prep)
Goal: Fetch yesterday's sales transactions from Salesforce (connector: Salesforce).
Write to /workspace/pipeline/raw_transactions_{YYYY-MM-DD}.csv

# Task 2 — runs daily at 8 AM (analysis)
Goal: Read /workspace/pipeline/raw_transactions_{YYYY-MM-DD}.csv
Compute revenue by region and product line.
Write to /workspace/pipeline/daily_analysis_{YYYY-MM-DD}.md

# Task 3 — runs daily at 9 AM (distribution)
Goal: Read /workspace/pipeline/daily_analysis_{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
Format as an executive summary (3 bullets, metrics table).
Post summary to Slack #sales-ops channel (connector: Slack).
Write confirmation to /workspace/logs/distribution-log.md
Stagger start times to give each stage enough time to complete before the next stage reads its output. For most tasks, 30-60 minutes of buffer between stages is sufficient.
Monitoring and Troubleshooting
Cowork does not send alerts when a scheduled task fails. You detect failures by checking the workspace output files.
Run Log Pattern
Have every scheduled task append a one-line entry to a run log so you can see at a glance whether recent runs completed.

# Append to the end of every scheduled task goal:
Append one line to /workspace/logs/run-log.md:
'Run completed: {YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC}, {N} records processed, output: {filename}'

If the task encountered an error or empty data, append:
'Run partial: {YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC}, reason: {brief description}'

# Example run-log.md contents:
Run completed: 2024-06-30 07:03 UTC, 142 records processed, output: 2024-06-30.md
Run completed: 2024-06-29 07:01 UTC, 138 records processed, output: 2024-06-29.md
Run partial: 2024-06-28 07:04 UTC, reason: Jira connector timeout, 0 records retrieved
Run completed: 2024-06-27 07:02 UTC, 155 records processed, output: 2024-06-27.md
Common Failure Modes
Failure
Symptom
Fix
Connector token expired
Output file missing or contains connector error message
Re-authorize the connector in project settings; re-run the task manually
Source file path changed
Goal references old path; output is empty or error
Update the path in the scheduled task goal; test with a manual run first
Output file not overwritten (append bug)
Output grows unbounded across runs
Verify goal says 'overwrite' not 'append' for the latest.md output
Race condition in pipeline
Stage 2 reads empty file (Stage 1 not finished)
Increase the gap between pipeline stage scheduled times
Stale baseline causes bad WoW diff
Comparison uses months-old data as 'last week'
Delete the stale baseline file; let the next run treat it as a first run
Pausing and Modifying Scheduled Tasks

Cowork lets you pause a scheduled task without deleting it. Use pause when the source data is temporarily unavailable (a connector migration, a data freeze) and you want to resume the same task later without re-configuring the schedule.

To modify the goal of a running scheduled task, edit the goal text in the Cowork interface and save. The change takes effect on the next scheduled run — it does not re-run the task immediately. Test the modified goal with a manual run before the next scheduled cycle to verify it handles the current workspace state correctly.
Always test a modified scheduled task goal with a manual run on a copy of the workspace state. A goal that worked last month may fail if the workspace structure changed since the last run.
Conditional Execution in Scheduled Goals

Some workflows should only produce output when certain conditions are met — data has changed, a threshold is crossed, or a new record appears. Conditional execution logic in the goal prevents noisy outputs on runs where nothing meaningful happened.
Change-Gated Output

A change-gated goal compares the current data to a stored snapshot and only produces a full report when something has changed. On no-change runs, it writes a brief 'no change' note rather than a full report.

Step 1: Read /workspace/state/last_snapshot.json.
If it does not exist, this is the first run — treat all data as new.

Step 2: Fetch current issue counts from Jira by status
(connector: Jira, groupBy: status, project: ANALYTICS).

Step 3: Compare current counts to last_snapshot.json.
If all counts are identical, write to /workspace/logs/run-log.md:
  'No change detected — skipping full report ({date})'
And stop.

Step 4 (if counts changed): Produce a markdown report showing
current counts, changes from snapshot, and which statuses changed.
Output to /workspace/outputs/latest.md and /workspace/outputs/{date}.md.

Step 5: Overwrite /workspace/state/last_snapshot.json with current counts.
Threshold Alert Goals
A threshold goal produces output only when a metric crosses a defined limit. This is useful for monitoring tasks that should alert on anomalies rather than report on normal operation.

Fetch yesterday's error rate from the analytics connector
(metric: server_error_rate, date: yesterday, granularity: daily).

If error rate < 2%:
  Append one line to /workspace/logs/health-log.md:
  '{date}: error rate {N}% — within normal range'
  Stop.

If error rate >= 2%:
  Write a detailed alert report to /workspace/outputs/alert-{date}.md:
  - Current error rate
  - Breakdown by endpoint (top 5 error sources)
  - Comparison to 7-day average
  - Suggested investigation steps based on the pattern
  Also append to /workspace/logs/health-log.md:
  '{date}: ALERT — error rate {N}% exceeds 2% threshold'
Using Connectors Effectively in Scheduled Tasks

Connectors are what make scheduled tasks genuinely useful: they bring fresh external data into every run without manual file uploads. Each connector call is scoped at execution time, not at schedule configuration time.
Scoping Connector Calls by Time

Most scheduled tasks need data from the previous period. Write the connector scope in terms of relative time ('last 7 days', 'yesterday', 'since last Monday') rather than absolute dates so the goal works correctly on every run cycle.

# Works on every weekly run:
Fetch Slack messages from #analytics-team from the last 7 days
(connector: Slack, channel: analytics-team, date_range: last_7_days)

# Breaks after first run (hardcoded date):
Fetch Slack messages from #analytics-team from 2024-06-23 to 2024-06-30
(connector: Slack, channel: analytics-team, date_range: 2024-06-23 to 2024-06-30)
Connector Permission Scope

Connector OAuth grants are set at the project level and apply to all tasks in the project, including scheduled ones. If a scheduled task needs to read a Google Drive folder that was not included in the original OAuth grant, the task will fail silently on that step. Verify connector permissions before enabling a scheduled task that relies on them.
Connector
Relative Time Parameter Pattern
Example
Google Analytics
date_range: last N days / yesterday / this week
date_range: last 7 days
Jira
updated >= -7d / created >= -1d
filter: updated >= -7d, project: ANALYTICS
Slack
date_range: last N days / since {relative}
date_range: last 14 days, channel: team-updates
Google Drive
modified after: {relative date}
modified after: 7 days ago, folder: /reports
GitHub
since: {ISO date relative}
since: 1 week ago, repo: org/analytics
Scheduled Tasks vs Manual Tasks: When to Use Each
Not every recurring task benefits from automation. The decision depends on how predictable the trigger is and how much the goal changes between runs.
Factor
Scheduled Task
Manual Task
Trigger
Fixed calendar interval
Event-driven or ad hoc
Goal stability
Same goal works every run
Goal changes based on current context
Data freshness
Connector or workspace provides fresh data on each run
Source data requires human judgment to select
Output consumer
Dashboard, log file, or downstream pipeline stage
Human-reviewed one-off deliverable
Error tolerance
Partial failure acceptable; next run will retry
Failure requires immediate attention
Setup cost
Higher (schedule config, workspace layout, state files)
Lower (write goal, run, done)

Schedule tasks that are genuinely repetitive with predictable data sources. If you find yourself editing the scheduled goal more than once a month, the task may be better handled as a manual run with a goal template.
Summary
Scheduled tasks run automatically on a repeating interval; the goal text is static but the data it operates on changes each run via connectors and workspace updates.
Design stateless goals for tasks that need no cross-run memory; use stateful goals with baseline files for trend comparison and incremental processing.
The workspace is the continuity layer: prior run outputs persist and are visible to the next cycle.
Write outputs to both a date-stamped archive file and a 'latest' file to maintain history without bloating the workspace.
Handle missing data and first-run conditions explicitly in the goal — the engine will not skip a scheduled run because data is unavailable.
Pipeline multiple scheduled tasks by staggering start times and having each stage read the prior stage's output from the workspace.
Maintain a run log file to detect silent failures without relying on manual output inspection.
